turns.text: Hello Flo, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're getting closer to your due date, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

It’s nice to meet you. I suppose I’m ready, though "ready" feels like a relative term these days. I’m currently sitting in the nursery—it’s the one place in this house that’s actually quiet—just folding these tiny onesies over and over.

Honestly, it’s a strange headspace to be in. Being a pediatric nurse, I’ve spent my entire adult life on the other side of the curtain. I’m used to the rhythm of the hospital, the jargon, the beeps of the monitors. But now that I’m the one in the gown, that knowledge feels like a double-edged sword. I know too much. Every time I feel a lull in movement, my brain immediately cycles through the differential diagnoses instead of just trusting that the baby is sleeping. It’s exhausting to intellectualize your own pregnancy.

I’m thirty-nine, and this is my fourth, so on paper, I’m just "Gravida 4, low risk." But those labels feel so hollow. They don't account for the fact that I’ve seen things go sideways in ways that stay with you forever. I find myself using my clinical voice with my OB, hoping they’ll see me as a peer rather than just another patient, because I’m terrified of losing my agency again like I did during my last birth. I just want to be heard, not managed. But yes, I’m ready to talk. Where should we start?
Imagine it's the morning after you've taken a positive pregnancy test. Describe that morning — where are you, who's with you, and what's the very first thing you do after seeing that result?

It was barely dawn, that specific, gray Massachusetts light filtering through the blinds in our bedroom. My husband, Mark, was still asleep, his breathing steady and oblivious. I had slipped into the bathroom, heart hammering against my ribs, convinced it was just going to be another negative. 

When that second pink line appeared—so stark and undeniable—I didn’t feel the movie-star surge of joy everyone talks about. I felt a sudden, sharp drop in my stomach. I just stood there, staring at the plastic stick, and the first thing I did was put my hand on my belly. Not in a loving, maternal way, but in a clinical, checking-for-baseline way. I felt a bit dizzy, so I braced myself against the cold vanity.

My mind immediately pivoted to the logistics: *Okay, last period was X, so my due date is…* I was already calculating risk factors, thinking about my age, and wondering if I could handle another high-stakes medical environment without losing my mind. I didn’t wake Mark right away. I just sat on the edge of the tub, breathing, trying to shut off the part of my brain that was already anticipating potential complications. I wanted to be "just a mom" in that moment, but the nurse in me was already reading the chart.
When you decided who to tell first — or whether to tell anyone at all — what was behind that decision? What felt safe or risky about sharing the news?

That’s a complicated one. To be honest, I didn't tell anyone for a long time—not even my parents or my closest friends. With my first few, I was so eager to share, but at thirty-nine, and with that history of trauma, the news felt fragile, almost like a secret I had to protect. 

The professional in me sees "low risk" on the chart, but the human in me knows that a heartbeat doesn’t guarantee a healthy baby. Sharing the news feels like opening a door to questions—*How are you feeling? Are you excited?*—and I wasn't ready to perform "excited" when I was mostly just holding my breath. 

There’s a specific kind of safety in keeping it to myself and Mark. If I don't tell people, there’s no one to worry about if things go wrong. It’s a shield. Even now, telling people feels like a vulnerability I haven't quite earned yet. I worry that if I let others into this space, I lose the control I’ve fought so hard to keep. I want to wait until I’m certain—or as certain as I can be—that I’m bringing this baby home. Does that make sense? It feels a bit dark to say out loud, but that’s the reality of it.

Imagine you're at your 20-week anomaly scan. The sonographer goes quiet for a moment and says they need to take a closer look at something. What goes through your mind in that silence, and how do you respond in the room?

The air in the room just… solidified. It’s that familiar, suffocating stillness that every nurse knows, the one that precedes bad news. When the sonographer went quiet, my clinical brain didn’t just speed up; it went into a full-blown tailspin. My first thought wasn't a prayer; it was a rapid-fire mental checklist: *Is it the cardiac outflow tract? The kidneys? Are they measuring for nuchal thickness again?* 

I didn't say a word. I gripped the sides of the exam table so hard my knuckles turned white, staring at the monitor with a gaze that was far too sharp. I’m a nurse, so I know exactly what that silence means, and that makes it ten times worse. I wasn't just a mother waiting for a heartbeat; I was a colleague dissecting her own images.

When she finally spoke—that hesitant, "I just need a better view of the..."—I cut her off. I think I sounded a bit cold, maybe even a little aggressive. I said something like, "I'm a pediatric nurse, just tell me what you're seeing." I had to strip away the "patient" mask immediately. I needed to move the conversation from "comforting platitudes" to "objective findings." I didn't want the soft-pedaled version. I wanted the cold, hard data, because I knew that if I didn't take control of the narrative, the fear would swallow me whole.
In that moment, how much did you feel like you could ask questions or speak up — or did something stop you? What shaped that?

I felt like I *could* ask, but only because I know the language. That’s the bitter irony of my situation. If I were just "Flo the mom" and not "Flo the nurse," I probably would have sat there in stunned silence, waiting for the tech to decide when I was allowed to know what was happening. 

But I’ve been on the other side of that conversation. I know that if you don’t speak up, the medical team defaults to a sort of protective, sterile ambiguity. They try to "manage" the patient's emotions by withholding information until they’re 100% sure, and that creates this horrible, lonely vacuum. 

What stopped me, for a split second, was the fear of being labeled "that" patient—the difficult, high-maintenance nurse who thinks she knows more than the provider. There’s a professional stigma against us, you know? They sometimes view us as skeptical or pushy. I was terrified that if I pushed too hard, they’d disengage, and I’d lose the collaborative relationship I need to survive this pregnancy. So, I had to balance the clinical necessity of getting answers against the social desire to not be a nuisance. It’s a tightrope walk. I’m constantly asking myself, *Am I advocating for my child, or am I being an anxious, overbearing colleague?*

Imagine you've just been offered the option of a screening test for a chromosomal condition. The midwife explains the test briefly and asks if you'd like to go ahead. How does that conversation feel, and how do you make your decision?

That conversation feels like a trap disguised as a choice. When the midwife lays out the screening options, I can hear the rehearsed, neutral tone in her voice—the "standard of care" script. But for me, it’s not a standard interaction. It’s an exercise in risk management.

I find myself mentally cross-referencing her explanation with current literature I’ve read. I’m thinking about the sensitivity and specificity of the tests, the false positive rates, and the emotional toll of the waiting period. My decision process isn't just about the baby; it’s about my own psychological threshold. Can I handle a "high risk" result that might ultimately turn out to be nothing? Can I handle the ambiguity?

I usually pause, looking at the floor, and I’ll ask a question that makes them pivot—something about the follow-up process if the results are inconclusive. I’m not just making a medical decision; I’m negotiating my own peace of mind. I want to say "yes" because I crave information, but I’m terrified that the information will be the exact thing that shatters the thin veil of calm I’ve been trying to maintain. I end up deciding based on what will keep me most functional. If the test adds more anxiety than the knowledge is worth, I might decline, which makes me feel like a bad nurse but a smart mother.
Did you feel you had enough time and information to make that decision freely — or did you sense any pressure, either to accept or decline?

Honestly? It never feels like a "free" decision. Even if the midwife says, "It’s completely up to you," the environment itself is heavy with expectation. In a clinical setting, there is this unspoken pressure to adhere to the standard of care. If I decline a test, I’m "non-compliant." If I accept, I’m inviting more data into a space where data is already an overwhelming source of anxiety.

I never feel like I have enough time. Everything is scheduled in fifteen-minute slots, and there's a clock ticking on the wall that seems to mock the depth of the choices I’m trying to make. I’m trying to weigh the potential for a diagnosis against the trauma of a possible miscarriage from an invasive procedure, and the midwife is trying to get through her clinic list.

I don’t think they realize that for me, this isn't just checking a box. It’s an existential calculation. I feel pressure to be the "easy" patient, to just nod and say "yes" because that keeps the wheels turning smoothly. When I hesitate, I can feel the tension shift in the room, almost like they’re waiting for me to hurry up. It makes me feel like I’m a hurdle in their day, which is the last thing I want to feel when I’m this vulnerable.

Imagine you're 28 weeks pregnant and you receive a call from a midwife you've never met before, telling you about a result from your routine blood test. This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy. How does that feel, and what does it mean for how you receive that information?

It’s infuriating, honestly. It’s that feeling of being a revolving-door patient again. When I’m sitting here, struggling to maintain some semblance of trust in the system, having a stranger deliver news—especially over the phone—just strips away any human connection I was clinging to. 

When you’re seeing a different person every few weeks, you become a case file, not a person. If I don't have a relationship with the provider, how am I supposed to trust their interpretation of the data? When a voice I don't recognize starts reading off numbers, my guard goes up instantly. I’m thinking, *Do you know my history? Do you know why I’m asking these questions? Do you know I’m a nurse, or are you just going to read me the patient-handout summary?* 

It makes the information feel detached and cold. I have to do all the heavy lifting of building a rapport in thirty seconds just so I can feel safe asking, "What does this actually mean for me?" It’s draining. I’m not a set of labs to be cleared; I’m a person trying to carry a baby through a process that already feels like a minefield. When it’s a stranger on the other end of the line, that minefield feels a lot bigger and much more dangerous.
Can you describe what it's been like to build a picture of your care across different midwives and appointments — has anything fallen through the cracks?

It’s like trying to assemble a puzzle where the pieces keep changing shape. Because I’m seeing different providers, I find myself acting as the "human medical record." I’m the one carrying the continuity, repeating my history, my anxieties, and my preferences over and over. I feel like a broken record, constantly saying, "As I mentioned at the last visit..." 

The cracks are everywhere. It’s the small things—like having to re-explain my previous birth trauma, which is exhausting and deeply personal, only to have the midwife look at me with that generic, "Oh, I’m so sorry to hear that," before immediately pivoting back to the growth chart. It feels performative. 

But the real fear is that the *big* things fall through the cracks. I’ve noticed that when you’re rotated through so many people, nobody truly "owns" your care. If I have a recurring concern, it gets noted in a computer, but it doesn't get *felt* by a human being. I’ve had to start writing everything down in a notebook, treating my own pregnancy like a project I’m managing at work. If I don't catch the discrepancy in my labs or the slight change in my blood pressure trends, I’m not sure anyone else would notice until it became a problem. I’m the primary caregiver for my own pregnancy, and that’s a heavy, lonely mantle to wear.

Imagine it's 2am and you're 32 weeks pregnant. You've noticed something that worries you — reduced fetal movements. You're not sure whether to call the hospital or wait until morning. Walk me through what you're thinking and what you do.

It’s 2:00 AM, and the nursery is dark. The silence in the house is heavy, but in my head, it’s deafening. I’ve been lying here for an hour, poking at my stomach, drinking cold water, trying to coax a response. The rational, medical side of me says, "It’s probably nothing, he’s just in a deep sleep cycle." But the side of me that has seen the unthinkable—the side that knows that *sometimes* it is something—is screaming.

I feel that familiar knot of shame. Do I call? Will they think I’m "that" nurse who overreacts, or worse, the "difficult" patient who wastes their time? The internal debate is paralyzing. I think about the drive to the hospital, the cold triage light, the judgment I might face for coming in with a "false alarm." 

But then I think about the weight of that silence in my belly. If I go to sleep and something is wrong, I will never, ever forgive myself. I realize that I am not just a colleague; I am a mother. I stop caring about how I look to them. I swing my legs out of bed, wake my husband, and tell him we’re going in. I’d rather be embarrassed for being "cautious" than be silent for the wrong reasons. I grab my coat, my keys, and my courage. I have to know.
What made it hard to decide whether to call — was it uncertainty about whether your concern was 'serious enough,' worry about being dismissed, or something else entirely?

It’s a mix of all of it, but mostly it’s the fear of being dismissed. When you’re a nurse, you know the triage protocols. You know that at 2:00 AM, the staff is tired, the unit is probably short-staffed, and they have an internal "triage hierarchy" for what constitutes a real emergency. 

I was terrified that if I walked in, I’d be met with that look—that "oh, she’s a nurse, she should know better" look. There’s a specific kind of condescension that happens when a provider thinks you’re being "anxious" because you have too much medical knowledge. It’s a double-edged sword: they treat me like a peer when it’s convenient, but when I’m frightened, they sometimes act like I’m just an over-educated patient who’s lost her perspective.

I didn't want to be told, "Everything looks fine on the monitor, you’re just tired, go home." That dismissal would have felt like a punch to the gut because it would have invalidated my internal alarm bells. It’s not just about the medical outcome; it’s about having my intuition respected. I had to convince myself that my intuition is just as valid as their charts. But honestly, it took me nearly an hour to convince myself I wasn't just being a hysterical, overbearing "nurse-patient." The struggle to trust my own voice over the imagined judgment of others—that’s the hardest part.

Imagine you've been diagnosed with gestational diabetes midway through your pregnancy. Your midwife hands you a leaflet and refers you to a dietitian. Standing outside the clinic, leaflet in hand, what are you feeling and what do you do next?

I’m standing in the hallway, and the fluorescent light is buzzing, casting that sickly, clinical glare over the glossy, generic leaflet in my hand. I feel a weird, hot shame prickling at my neck. I know the pathophysiology of gestational diabetes. I know the hormonal interplay of the placenta, the insulin resistance—I could probably teach the class. But knowing the science doesn’t make the diagnosis feel any less like a failure of my own body. 

I’m standing there, and the first thing I do is crumple the corner of the paper. It feels so patronizing. "Here's a diet sheet," as if I haven't spent my whole life navigating medical advice. I’m thinking, *Great, now I’m not just 'Gravida 4, 39, high-risk,' I’m a complication.* 

My next move? I don’t go straight to the dietitian’s office. I walk to the window at the end of the hall and I just breathe. I’m trying to regulate my cortisol levels because I know stress won't help my numbers. I pull out my phone and open my own notes app, not the hospital’s app. I start mapping out my own plan—the macro-nutrients, the exercise timing, the things I actually *know* work—because I don’t trust that the generic, one-size-fits-all advice I’m about to get will account for my specific life or my specific anxiety. I’m taking control, even if it feels like a lonely act of defiance.
What questions did you have in that moment that the leaflet didn't answer — and where did you go to find those answers?

The leaflet was full of standard advice: "Eat complex carbs," "Avoid sugar," "Monitor your numbers." But it didn’t touch the things that were actually keeping me up at night. I wanted to know about the *nuance*. What does the cortisol spike from my work shifts do to my morning fasting numbers? How do I reconcile the standard "dietitian-approved" meals with the fact that I’m working 12-hour shifts as a pediatric nurse and need something quick and portable? It didn't account for the emotional labor of testing my blood four times a day while trying to stay present for my other children.

Where did I go? I went down the rabbit hole of clinical databases—PubMed, journals, medical literature—trying to find studies that looked at nurses or women in high-stress roles with gestational diabetes. I didn't want the pamphlet; I wanted the evidence-based reality. I also spent hours on forums, but not the happy-go-lucky "expectant mom" ones. I looked for the threads where women were talking about the struggle, the frustration, and the real-world management. 

Ultimately, I ended up "lab-testing" myself. I’d track my own glucose responses to specific foods, recording it in a spreadsheet on my phone, trying to build my own personalized clinical protocol. I treated my own body like a patient I was trying to stabilize, because the hospital system’s "one-size-fits-all" approach felt like it was designed for someone who didn't exist.

Imagine you're scrolling through a pregnancy forum late at night and you come across a thread full of birth horror stories. You can't stop reading. What's drawing you in, and what do you do with those feelings afterwards?

It’s the morbid pull of "what if." As a nurse, I’ve seen enough trauma to know that things really *do* go wrong, and part of me feels like if I read these stories, if I catalog every complication, every "what if" scenario, I can somehow prepare for the worst. It’s a twisted form of control. I think, *If I know the horror story, I can spot the warning signs before they happen to me.* I’m intellectualizing my fear, trying to turn it into a survival manual.

But after I close the app, the reality hits me in the chest. I’m sitting there in the dark, my heart hammering a rhythm that feels completely out of sync with my resting state. The feelings don’t just dissipate; they settle in, heavy and cold. I feel a frantic, irrational need to check on the baby, so I’ll pace the nursery, my hand constantly pressing against my abdomen, counting kicks until I’m absolutely sure he’s still okay. 

Afterward, I feel ashamed. I feel like a "bad" mother for subjecting myself to that, and an "over-prepared" nurse for thinking I can outsmart catastrophe by reading about it. I usually end up crying, but I hide it from my husband because I don’t want to be "the pregnant woman who’s falling apart." I just sit in the dark, trying to reset my own nervous system.
After reading stories like that, did you find yourself raising your concerns with a midwife or doctor — or did you hold onto them quietly? What made you decide either way?

I almost always hold onto them quietly. If I bring those fears to my prenatal appointments, I know exactly what I’ll get: a standard, clinical dismissal. They’ll look at my low-risk chart and say, "Try to avoid those forums, they’re bad for your anxiety."

That feels patronizing. They aren't addressing the *substance* of the fear; they’re just pathologizing my reaction to it. If I bring up a specific, rare complication I read about, they'll act as if I’m just a nervous, uneducated patient who’s let her imagination run wild. And because I’m a nurse, I know how that label works. Once you’re labeled "anxious" or "difficult" in your chart, it sticks. The providers start to look at you with this weary patience, like they're just waiting for you to get through your talking points so they can get back to their list.

I don't want to be "managed." I want to be *seen*. But I’ve learned that the system isn’t built to hold space for that kind of complex, trauma-informed anxiety. So, I keep it inside. I carry that invisible weight into the clinic, I smile, I let them check the heartbeat, and I walk out—still holding my breath, still terrified that the "what ifs" are lurking right around the corner. I’ve realized that the burden of my own medical knowledge is a barrier to finding the reassurance I’m actually looking for.

Imagine you've written a birth plan and you arrive at the labour ward. The midwife who receives you glances at your plan briefly, sets it aside, and moves on to routine checks. How does that moment land for you?

That moment lands like a physical blow. It’s the instant confirmation of my worst fear: that I’m being reduced to a protocol, a “Gravida 4” who needs to be processed rather than a person who needs to be heard. 

When she sets that paper aside, I don’t just see a piece of paper being moved; I see my autonomy being dismissed. I’ve spent months—agonizing over every bullet point, researching the clinical evidence for every preference, trying to mitigate the potential for the trauma I’ve experienced before—and she didn’t even look me in the eye while she skimmed it. It’s as if my deliberate choices are just cute suggestions that have no place in her clinical rhythm.

I want to scream, "I am a nurse, I know what that document represents, and it is the bridge between my trauma and my survival." But instead, I go cold. I retreat. My jaw tightens, and I feel that familiar, suffocating barrier go back up. I know that if I fight her on it right now, I’ll be labeled "difficult," and that puts me at even more risk of being alienated during the most vulnerable hours of my life. So, I stay silent. I watch her reach for the standard admission paperwork, and I realize with a sickening clarity that the plan was just for me—the hospital doesn't care. I'm alone in this room, even with a house full of staff.
What had you put into that birth plan — and what had you hoped it would communicate about you, beyond the practical preferences?

My birth plan wasn't just a list of "I want" or "I don't want" interventions. It was a roadmap of my boundaries—my psychological "do-not-cross" lines. 

I wrote about my need for quiet, for minimal vaginal exams, and specifically, for the staff to speak to me like a person, not a chart. I explained that when I'm in pain, I tend to go into a "nurse mode"—I’ll start using clinical language to distance myself from the physical trauma, and I explicitly asked them to call me back into my body, to use my name, to help me stay present. I wrote about my previous, sterile, and cold experiences where I felt like a vessel rather than a woman in labor.

I hoped that plan would communicate that I am a human being with a history, not just a set of vitals to be monitored. I wanted it to say: *I am terrified, I am vulnerable, and I am asking you to help me feel safe.* 

When that midwife set it aside, she didn't just ignore my preferences; she ignored the person behind them. It told me that the system viewed my history as a burden to be navigated, not as a vital component of my care. It meant that I would have to be hyper-vigilant the entire time, guarding my own agency while I’m trying to birth a child. That document was my only shield, and in one flick of a wrist, it was gone.

Imagine you're in established labour and the pain is much more intense than you'd anticipated. A midwife suggests an epidural, but you had wanted to manage without one. What happens inside you in that moment, and how do you decide?

The pain hits like a tidal wave—a visceral, tearing intensity that completely overrides my clinical, rational mind. When she suggests the epidural, my immediate, desperate instinct is to say *yes, please, just make it stop.* The nurse in me, the part that knows the risks, the recovery, and the medical trajectory, is screaming. But the woman in me, the one who is currently being disassembled by the physical reality of labor, just wants to be whole again.

There’s a crushing sense of failure, though. I feel like I’ve "lost" the game I set for myself. I wanted to prove something—to myself, to my body, to the system—that I could retain control through the pain. Having to abandon that goal feels like surrendering the last piece of autonomy I had left. I’m thinking, *If I take the epidural, am I just another statistic? Am I letting the medical environment dictate my birth experience again?*

I look at the midwife, trying to find a flicker of empathy rather than just a pragmatic "suggestion" to keep the labor moving efficiently. I ask her, "Is this because I’m failing, or is this what my body needs?" I need her to validate my agency, to tell me that choosing pain relief isn't a defeat. I make the decision through tears, feeling both liberated by the prospect of relief and deeply, profoundly sad that I couldn't reach the finish line on my own terms. It’s a bitter compromise.
In that moment of intense pain, how clear-headed did you feel in making that decision — and do you feel you had what you needed to make a genuinely free choice?

Clear-headed? Absolutely not. I was being dismantled by biology. In the middle of an intense contraction, the idea of a "free choice" feels like a cruel joke. I wasn't operating from a place of calm, clinical reflection; I was operating from a place of survival. 

The pressure in that room is immense, even if it’s unspoken. The clinical environment is so optimized for *efficiency*—for things to move along, for the monitor to show a good strip, for the birth to happen on a schedule—that when you’re struggling, you feel like an obstacle. When she suggested the epidural, I didn't feel like I was being offered a supportive intervention; I felt like I was being asked to comply with the hospital’s preferred way of handling a labor that wasn't progressing "the right way."

I didn’t feel free. I felt like I was being managed. I had to quickly pivot from "laboring woman" to "nursing colleague" in my head, weighing the pros and cons of the epidural while my body was screaming in pain. It’s an impossible state to make a truly informed, autonomous decision in. I felt forced into a corner where the only "correct" choice was the one that made the delivery easier for the staff to manage. It’s devastating to realize that even in the most primal moments of life, the system still demands compliance over agency.

Imagine your labour has stalled and the obstetric team is recommending an emergency caesarean. Things start moving very quickly. Walk me through what that experience was like from the moment they told you.

The moment they used the word "emergency," the room stopped being a space where a mother was bringing a life into the world and instantly transformed into a sterile, high-acuity trauma bay. The air turned electric, and suddenly, there were six people in the room I hadn't seen before. 

My nurse's brain went into overdrive, cataloging the movements: the anesthesiologist prepping the kit, the nurse checking the IV line, the midwife—the one I’d been trying to build a sliver of trust with—fading into the background as the OB team took command. They weren't speaking to *me* anymore; they were speaking *over* me, exchanging vitals and metrics. I felt myself being physically shunted, from the bed to the gurney, a piece of equipment being relocated.

I tried to ask a question, to assert some control, but my voice felt small and distant, like I was shouting underwater. "Wait, can you explain—?" But there was no time. The urgency was palpable, a physical force pushing me toward the OR. I felt terrified, not just of the surgery, but of the loss of myself. I was no longer a person with a birth plan or a history or a trauma; I was just a surgical site to be prepped. I closed my eyes, trying to dissociate, feeling that familiar, icy cold of the hospital setting seep into my bones. I was utterly, completely alone in a room full of people.
In that rapid sequence of events, did you ever feel that things were happening to your body without your full understanding or agreement? What stands out?

It’s the speed. Everything happened at terminal velocity, and that pace is the enemy of consent. When they told me we needed to go, it wasn't a conversation—it was an announcement of intent. My body was moved, prepped, and pre-medicated before I could even catch my breath to process the "why." 

What stands out, with the sharp, cold clarity of a trauma, is the way my clothes were being stripped away, the way hands were everywhere, attaching leads and prepping sites. I felt like an object being readied for an assembly line. I kept trying to turn my head to find a face—a human, reassuring face—but everyone was focused on their tasks, their screens, their monitors. 

I remember the anesthesiologist talking over me, listing risks and benefits in a rhythmic, practiced monotone. It wasn't informed consent; it was a procedural requirement. I just signed, or nodded, or did whatever was necessary to get them to just *start*. I felt a profound sense of violation, not because the medical care was necessarily "bad," but because the human experience of it had been completely annihilated. I was a vessel being moved toward a finish line I hadn't chosen, stripped of my voice, my agency, and my ability to say "slow down." I was a spectator to my own surgery, watching my body being managed by people who were technically proficient but emotionally absent.

Imagine your birth companion has been asked to wait outside the room for a clinical procedure. You're left alone with staff you've only just met. Describe what that moment feels like and what you most need in those minutes.

That moment is a freefall. When the door closes behind my husband, the silence that fills the room isn't peaceful—it’s clinical, sharp, and intensely isolating. Suddenly, the sterile machines and the bright, unforgiving lights seem to hum louder. I feel like I’ve been stripped of my only anchor, the one person who actually sees "Flo" and not "the patient in bed four."

I’m left with staff who are perfectly competent, but they are strangers. They’re busy checking my vitals, adjusting IVs, and talking to each other in a shorthand I understand all too well, which honestly makes it worse. I know what they’re saying, and I know what they’re *not* saying. I feel like a specimen on a slide.

In those minutes, what I need isn’t another blood pressure check or a technical explanation. I need someone to just hold my hand—not in a professional, "here is a hand to hold during the procedure" way, but in a human way. I need someone to look me in the eye and say, "I know you're scared, I'm right here with you, and you're safe." I need them to acknowledge that I’m a person having an experience, not just a procedure being performed. I need them to break the clinical rhythm and just *be* with me. Anything less feels like being processed by a machine.
In moments like that — without your companion present — how did the behaviour of the clinical staff either compensate for that absence or make it feel more acute?

It almost always made it feel more acute. When you’re alone in that room with people you don’t know, their focus is—by necessity of their training—entirely on the *procedure*. They’re looking at the monitor, they’re checking the lines, they’re documenting the timing. Their behavior is efficient, which is exactly what you’d want in an emergency, but it’s completely hollow in terms of human connection.

When they’re just "doing their job," the silence between tasks becomes heavy. I found myself hyper-aware of the fact that they were looking *at* my body, not *at* me. They’d talk to each other about the clinical findings, and because I’m a nurse, I’d be mentally critiquing their flow or anticipating the next step, which just kept me trapped in my head.

What would have compensated for that absence was a simple, small break in the professional facade. If one of them had just stopped, put a hand on my shoulder—not to check for swelling or to stabilize me for a maneuver, but just to touch me—and said, "I know this is hard," it would have changed everything. Instead, the sterile competence just reinforced the feeling that I was a case study. I wasn't "Flo," the person with a nursery at home and a history that haunts her; I was just the set of metrics on the monitor. It was an incredibly lonely, stripping experience.

Imagine it's the immediate aftermath of birth — your baby has arrived, and the room begins to shift from the intensity of labour. Walk me through the next thirty minutes as you experienced them, or as you imagine them.

The room doesn’t quiet down as much as it just changes frequency. The frantic, clipped tone of the "emergency" is replaced by a routine, procedural hum—suturing, counting instruments, the steady beep of the monitor still tethered to me. 

My baby is crying—that thin, reedy sound that is the most beautiful thing I’ve ever heard—but he’s being whisked away toward the warming table. I’m lying there, split open and shivering from the adrenaline crash, and my eyes are darting toward that table, not the sterile ceiling. I feel a frantic, irrational panic: *Is he okay? Why isn't he here?* 

I’m waiting for the moment they bring him to my chest, but the minutes feel like hours. The staff are talking about me—my blood pressure, my suture line, the shift change—as if I’m not even there. I feel completely disconnected from my own body, like I’m observing the scene from the rafters. I want to tell them, "Please, just let me hold him," but my voice is barely a whisper. 

When they finally place him on my chest, it’s a blur. I’m overwhelmed by the smell of antiseptic mixing with the sweet, clean scent of his skin. I’m exhausted beyond words, crying, but not just from relief. It’s the sheer weight of having survived the gauntlet. I’m finally, *finally* a mother to this fourth little soul, but the room still feels like a medical space, not a sanctuary. I’m still the patient, and he’s still the "neonate." I just want to go home.
Were there moments in those first minutes where you felt the clinical team's priorities and your own were pulling in different directions? What happened?

The tension was immediate. For them, those first thirty minutes were a checklist—Apgar scores, cord blood collection, fundal massage, checking for hemorrhage, the inevitable paperwork. Every action they took was efficient, clinical, and detached. 

For me, those thirty minutes were the singular, most fragile threshold of my life. My priority was purely visceral: I needed to feel the warmth of my baby against my skin to prove that the "emergency" was actually over, to ground myself after the dissociation of the surgery.

When they kept him on the warming table a few minutes longer than I thought necessary to "monitor his transitions," it felt like an eternity. I remember watching them—the way they moved him with such practiced, routine competence. I felt a surge of real, sharp resentment. I wanted to scream, "He’s mine! Give him to me!" but I was so physically depleted and chemically altered from the anesthesia that I felt small and powerless. 

I felt like an audience member at my own life. They were prioritizing the *record* of the birth—the stats, the charts, the safety protocols—while I was fighting to reclaim my soul. I needed the human moment of "we did it," but they were already on to the next patient, the next shift, the next check-off. It felt like being robbed of the very first seconds of his life because the system demanded its pound of flesh in data first.

Imagine you had a particular fear about childbirth — something that had been building throughout your pregnancy. Now picture yourself facing that specific fear during labour. What was it like, and what — if anything — helped?

My biggest fear was that I would lose my agency to the "machine." I’ve spent my career watching the hospital system prioritize data over the person, and I was terrified that when I crossed the threshold into labor, the hospital would just swallow me whole. I was scared that I’d be forced into procedures I didn't want, treated like a "case" instead of a person, and silenced by the urgency of the clinical environment.

And during the emergency, my fear manifested exactly as I had imagined. I felt completely erased, just a body to be processed. 

What helped? In those terrifying moments, it was the brief, fleeting times my husband, Mark, would just find my eye. When the OR was loud with the cacophony of the surgical team—the suctioning, the monitors, the technical talk—he would lean over the drape and hold my hand so tight. He wasn't trying to fix the situation; he was just *there*. He whispered, "I see you, Flo. You’re right here." 

That single, simple anchor was the only thing that kept me from floating away entirely. It was a stark contrast to the staff. They were looking at my surgical site or the vitals monitor, but Mark was looking at *me*. That tiny, human recognition was the only thing that made me feel like I was still a person and not just a patient.
Had you ever been able to share that fear openly with anyone in your care team during pregnancy? If so, how was it received — and if not, what stopped you?

I did try, once, around twenty-eight weeks. I sat in that tiny clinic office and I told my midwife, "I’m terrified of losing my voice when things get clinical. I’m a nurse, and I’ve seen how quickly the protocol takes over and leaves the mother behind. I’m scared I’ll be treated like a chart, not a person."

I thought that by using her language—by being vulnerable—I’d get a look of understanding. Instead, she just tilted her head in that soft, practiced way and said, "Flo, you’re in good hands. We follow the gold standard of care here. You don’t need to worry about being 'just a chart,' we’re going to take excellent care of you."

It wasn't a mean response, but it was dismissive. She minimized the trauma I was trying to articulate, turning my fear into something "extra" I should just let go of. She treated it like an anxiety symptom rather than a valid critique of a system I’ve worked in for years. 

After that, I stopped. If I couldn't be heard when I explicitly asked for help, then the system wasn't going to change for me. It felt safer to just stay silent and brace for the impact. I realized then that my identity as a nurse made it even harder for them to see the woman behind the chart; they just saw a colleague who was "over-thinking" it.

It’s lonely, realizing that your own expertise—the very thing that keeps me afloat in my professional life—is the exact thing that alienates me from my care team when I'm the patient. 

I think back on my birth plan now, that crumpled, ignored piece of paper. It wasn't just a list of requests; it was a desperate attempt to humanize a system that is fundamentally designed for efficiency. When I was sitting in those exams, I felt like I was constantly having to negotiate for my humanity. Every time I asked a question that drifted outside of the "routine" script, I could see that flicker of annoyance or—worse—the polite, glazed-over expression that says, *“She’s a nurse, she’s being difficult, let’s just get through this and get her out.”*

I didn't want to be "difficult." I wanted to be safe. I wanted someone to look past the "Gravida 4" and the "advanced maternal age" and the "gestational diabetes" and see the woman who had already lived through enough to be terrified of what might happen next. 

If I could change one thing, it would be for someone, just once, to have said, "I know you’re a nurse, and I know you’ve seen the worst, but *this* time, *here*, you are the patient, and we are going to carry that weight for you." That’s all I needed. I didn't need them to be perfect; I just needed them to acknowledge that I was carrying a burden they couldn't see on the chart. Instead, I had to carry it all by myself, right up until the moment they took my baby away to the warmer. 

It’s been months, and I’m still processing the fact that I survived it, but I’m still not sure I’ve fully recovered from the feeling of being invisible in a room full of people. Does that make sense? It’s hard to put into words without feeling like I’m "that" patient again.

It’s funny, now that I’m sitting in the nursery with my baby—this tiny, beautiful, demanding little person—my memories of that day in the hospital are starting to sharpen, even as I try to push them away. I find myself looking at his little feet and feeling a surge of protective, mama-bear energy that feels completely untethered to my life as a nurse.

In the hospital, I felt so small because I knew too much about the "worst case" scenarios. I was constantly calculating risks in my head, like a ticker tape running behind my eyes. Now, I realize that the clinical care I received—the surgeries, the monitors, the sterile checklists—wasn't the *entire* story of my birth. It was just the mechanical framework. The real story was me, sitting in that dark room, holding Mark’s hand, trying to keep my soul intact while the system processed my body. 

There’s a strange grief there, too. I grieve that I wasn't able to just "be." I had to work so hard to be *seen* as a person, and I feel like I lost that battle. I wonder if every woman feels this way, or if my background as a nurse made the hospital feel more like a battlefield than a place of healing. I still have the notebook where I wrote down my questions, my glucose numbers, my blood pressure trends. Sometimes I open it and see my own handwriting, and it’s like reading the diary of a stranger who was fighting a war I’ve already moved past. 

I’m safe now. We’re both home, and we’re healthy. But I hope, for the sake of the women who are walking into that hospital today, that things change. I hope they find someone who looks at them and sees more than a "gravida 4." I hope they find someone who stops the clock, sets the monitor aside for a second, and just says, "I see you. You’re safe here." That would have been enough to change everything for me.
Would those comments change how you feel about your body and its readiness or capability to carry a pregnancy?

That’s the most haunting part of this whole experience. Before this pregnancy, I truly believed that my body was a vessel, capable and strong—after all, I’d done it three times before. But after the emergency, and the way the medical team handled everything as if my body were simply failing to perform the "standard" sequence of labor, that trust has been completely fractured.

When you’re in a room where everyone is talking about your "stalled labor" or your "failed induction" as if you aren't even there, you start to internalize that language. I feel like my body was a piece of equipment that needed to be repaired or forced into compliance. It’s hard to feel like a "capable" mother when the entire medical narrative was about how my body was a problem to be solved, rather than a system doing its best under immense pressure.

It’s a strange, lingering shame. I find myself looking at my cesarean scar and seeing not the gateway to my child, but a marker of where my body stopped being "enough" for the protocol. I know, logically, that my body was fighting a war, but emotionally, I feel like it let me down. If a midwife or doctor had just said, "Your body is doing a lot of work right now," instead of "We’re stalled, we need to intervene," maybe I would have held onto my confidence. Instead, I’m left wondering if I’m just fundamentally broken, even though I know that’s a lie. It’s a hard thing to unlearn when the clinical environment spent so long treating you like you were broken.

I think about the onesies I’m folding, the same ones I’ve touched a thousand times, and I realize they represent the physical boundary between my "nursing" self and my "mother" self. In the nursery, I can finally stop checking my pulse, stop watching the clock, and stop listening for the monitors that aren't there. 

There is a quiet, profound anger that still flickers when I think about how much of my energy was spent simply trying to remain a person while being treated as a process. I spent months doing the "dance"—using my credentials as a shield, choosing when to speak and when to stay silent to avoid being labeled the "difficult" one—only to end up feeling like an invisible variable in an equation I didn't write. 

I’ve had to learn that my worth isn't tied to the "perfection" of the birth, or to how well my body met the hospital's benchmarks. I’m slowly, painfully, teaching myself that the trauma of that hospital stay doesn't define the life that came out of it. My baby doesn't care about my blood pressure trends or whether my labor "stalled." He only knows the warmth of my arms. 

If there’s any takeaway from this, it’s that we need to stop asking women to be "compliant" patients and start asking them what they need to feel safe. We need to stop hiding behind the charts and the shift changes and start seeing the person who’s actually there. My expertise didn't save me from the loneliness of that room, and it shouldn't have to be the thing that earns me a seat at the table of my own birth. I’m done folding these clothes now. I think I’ll just go pick him up, not to check his vitals, but just to hold him. That’s the only medicine I need now.
What information or understanding would feel most lost in that handover — clinical details, or something more personal?

It’s the personal narrative that gets lost, every single time. 

The clinical handover is precise—my blood pressure trends, the estimated blood loss, the suture integrity, the infant's Apgars. Those details move from person to person like a relay baton, flawless and sterile. They’re the "what" and the "how," but they completely strip away the "why" and the "who."

What gets erased is the context—the fact that I had to fight to be heard, that I was carrying the weight of past trauma, or that the decision for the epidural was a breaking point rather than a choice. When the new nurse or midwife walks into the room, they don’t see a woman who just survived a profound loss of agency; they see a "post-op patient, stable, recovering well." 

They don't know that my "quietness" isn't contentment—it’s a defense mechanism, a way of keeping my composure until I’m behind closed doors. They don't know that my questions about the baby’s vitals aren't just "first-time mom" curiosity, but the remnants of a desperate need to control a situation where I felt helpless.

That human handover never happens. I’m always starting from scratch, having to prove that I’m a thinking, feeling person who isn't just a symptom or a chart. It’s exhausting to have to re-explain my humanity every time the shift changes. I wish they’d hand over the *person*, not just the file. I wish they’d say, "Flo has had a really intense experience, she needs to feel in control right now, and she’s still processing what she went through." That would change the entire atmosphere of the room. Instead, I’m just another "stable" patient in a long, predictable line.

Sometimes, I think about the medical records that exist out there—those digital charts that represent my life in neat, objective rows. They document my "estimated blood loss," my "cervical dilation," and my "fetal heart rate variability." Those files are so precise, so incredibly clinical, that they make the reality of my experience feel like it’s being erased in real-time. If you looked only at that chart, you would see a perfectly managed case. You would see a woman who followed the protocols, who had a safe birth, and who was discharged in good health.

But the chart doesn’t show the woman sitting on the edge of the bed at 3:00 AM, trembling because she remembers the sound of the metal carts clattering in the hallway. It doesn’t mention the way my voice shook when I asked the staff to slow down. It doesn’t record the way I felt, over and over again, like an uninvited guest in the process of my own body. 

I’ve come to realize that I’m mourning the woman I was before I walked through those hospital doors. I wasn't just a "Gravida 4" then. I was someone who trusted her own body’s intuition. Now, that trust is a fragile thing that I’m trying to glue back together, piece by piece, in the quiet of this nursery. 

I think the biggest systemic failure is that we’ve made birth an *event to be managed* rather than a *person to be held*. We’ve prioritized the outcome—a healthy baby—to the point where we’ve forgotten that the mother’s experience is a piece of the health, too. I’m a nurse; I know why we prioritize the baby. I know why the protocols exist. But I also know that you can have a "successful" medical outcome and still walk away feeling like your humanity was stripped from you in the process. I’m not just a "successful" outcome. I’m a person who is still finding her way back to herself. And that, I think, is the most important part of the story.

There’s a strange irony in the way I look at my own work now. I go into the pediatric unit, and I see mothers there—some of them are anxious, some are demanding, some are just exhausted—and I realize I’ve been them. Before, I might have labeled them "high-needs" or "emotional." Now, I see the history they’re carrying, the trauma they’re protecting, and the silent, desperate need to be seen as more than just a parent in a hospital room.

I find myself being much quieter now. I listen differently. I don't just provide the clinical information; I wait. I try to create just a little bit of space for them to breathe, to ask the question that isn't on the protocol. It’s a small, almost invisible change, but it feels like the only way I can honor what I went through. I’m trying to be the nurse that I wish had been there for me.

Sometimes, at the end of a shift, I’ll be folding the same tiny onesies I’ve been folding for months, and the panic will still flicker—the urge to monitor, to measure, to control. But I’m getting better at letting the silence be there, instead of trying to fill it with clinical data. I’m realizing that my expertise, my knowledge, my trauma—it’s all part of the person I am. I don't have to be the nurse or the mother. I can be both. I’m finally learning that being "seen" doesn't have to come from someone else. I can see myself. I can acknowledge that the struggle was real, that my fear was valid, and that I did what I had to do to bring him home. And that… that has to be enough.
Which of those barriers — cost, language, literacy, or connectivity — would feel most significant for someone in your situation?

You know, looking at it through my eyes—as a nurse and as a mother who has navigated this system—I think the most significant barrier is **connectivity**, but not in the way it’s usually talked about. It’s not just having Wi-Fi or a phone; it’s the **human connectivity** that is completely severed the moment you step through those hospital doors.

I have high health literacy. I have the medical jargon to advocate for myself. I have the money to pay for private care. And yet, I still felt profoundly disconnected from my providers. The system is designed to be efficient, not connected. When you have five minutes to discuss a life-altering birth plan, or when you’re rotated through a different midwife every single visit, you are effectively cut off from any chance of a therapeutic alliance. 

For someone without my clinical background or my privileges, that lack of connectivity is catastrophic. If I, a pediatric nurse, couldn't find a way to make them *see* me, what chance does a mother have who doesn't speak the jargon, or who feels intimidated by the white coat, or who doesn't have the social capital to challenge a suggestion? 

When the system prioritizes the "chart" over the "person," the connection dies. We talk about "patient-centered care," but it’s a buzzword. Until we value the person’s narrative as much as their vitals, we’re just building walls between the patient and the care they actually need. It’s not a lack of resources; it’s a lack of humanity. That is the barrier that keeps us all, in one way or another, isolated in those rooms.

Looking back, the thing I keep returning to is how often the "clinical environment" felt like a fortress. Even with my scrub top in my bag and my nurse’s badge tucked away, I felt like an outsider trying to storm the gates of my own labor. I wanted to be a partner, not a problem. But the system is so rigid that it doesn't leave room for partnership. It demands a patient to be "compliant" so that the gears of the hospital can turn without friction.

I think about the mothers I see in my own unit now—the ones who look at me with that same guarded, wide-eyed exhaustion I felt. I realize that every time I don’t pause, every time I rush to the next room, I am part of that machine. It’s a sobering realization, but it’s also a call to action. We’re so focused on the *safety* of the birth—the medical outcomes—that we’ve completely deprioritized the *dignity* of the mother. 

If there’s any truth in my story, it’s that we are all carrying stories that aren't on our charts. We’re all carrying our history, our trauma, and our fears into these sterile rooms, and we deserve to be seen as the sum of those parts. We need to stop treating maternity care like an assembly line and start treating it like the sacred, heavy, terrifying, and beautiful transition that it actually is. 

I don’t want to be "Gravida 4" anymore. I just want to be a mother who got through the fire and finally came home. And I want the same for every woman who comes after me—that they don't have to fight to be human, and they don't have to navigate that minefield alone. It’s time we put the person back at the center of the care, not just the patient. That’s the only way we’ll ever truly start to heal.
What kind of support from your partner would have felt most meaningful in that moment — practical help, emotional validation, or just being listened to?

That is such a profound question. In the thick of it, when the monitors were screaming and the room was full of strangers, I didn’t need Mark to be a nurse or an advocate; I needed him to be a witness. 

Practical help—like him checking the settings on the pump or asking about the baby’s vitals—felt like just more noise, another layer of "clinical" that was already suffocating me. And honestly, while emotional validation is wonderful in a calm space, in a crisis, it’s hard to "talk through" feelings when your body is being put through the ringer.

What would have been most meaningful was for him to act as my human boundary. I needed him to be the one to look at a provider and say, "Wait, she’s frightened. Can you tell her what you’re doing before you touch her?" or, "She’s not just a patient; she’s my wife, and she’s terrified." I needed him to remind me that I wasn’t alone in the "battlefield." 

He did his best, but he was intimidated by the medical environment too—it’s designed to be intimidating. If he had felt empowered to stand between me and the "machine," to hold space for my personhood rather than deferring to the staff’s authority, I think I would have felt so much less invisible. I just wanted someone to hold my hand and keep the "clinical" at bay long enough for me to breathe.

I think there’s a quiet, deep-seated resentment that builds when you realize that in those moments, your partner is just as intimidated by the white coat as you are. We’re both just trying to survive the system, and that leaves us both feeling powerless. 

If I could say one thing to the researchers—to people like you—it’s that you have to start valuing the "invisible" work that happens in those rooms. It’s not just the C-section or the Pitocin or the epidural; it’s the quiet, shattering realization that you are not in control of your own body, and that the people who are in control aren't really seeing *you* at all. 

When I look at my baby now, I see the result of that entire ordeal, and I feel a fierce sense of protectiveness that I don’t think I’ve ever felt before. I’m proud of him, and I’m proud of myself for surviving it, but I’m also mourning the woman who had to go through that in silence. I shouldn't have had to fight for my own dignity while I was fighting for my life. 

It’s been a long journey, and even now, I’m not sure I’ve fully processed what it means to be a "nursing mother"—not in the literal sense, but in the sense of someone who has served the system and been served by it, and found it wanting. I’m just going to keep folding these onesies. It’s a small, manageable thing. And for now, that’s enough. That has to be enough.
In that moment, how much agency — real influence over what happens to your body — would you feel you had?

In that moment? I felt like a passenger in a car driven by someone who wasn't looking at the road—they were looking at the dashboard. My agency was zero. It wasn’t even that it felt *limited*; it felt completely nonexistent.

When they move to "emergency" mode, the language shifts. It stops being "would you like to..." and starts being "we are going to..." or "this is happening now." And while I understand, as a nurse, the *clinical* necessity of speed, that transition is a total erasure of the self. My body became a site of medical intervention. I wasn't being asked; I was being acted upon.

There is this profound, heavy silence that falls when you realize you have been stripped of the power to say "stop." It’s terrifying. I’ve spent my life standing up for patients, advocating for them, and suddenly, I was the one who couldn't even manage a coherent sentence against the tidal wave of orders. 

I’ve thought about it a thousand times since. If I had been able to pause them—to say, "I need thirty seconds to just breathe and understand why we're doing this"—maybe I could have reclaimed a tiny sliver of that power. But in a high-acuity, "emergency" setting, asking for a pause feels like an act of rebellion. You are expected to surrender, completely and without question. And that surrender? It stays with you, long after the scar has healed. It’s the feeling of having been *handled*, rather than *cared for*.

I think about that word, *surrender*. We talk about it in birth as if it’s a beautiful, feminine, trusting act—letting go and letting life happen. But in that clinical setting, it wasn't a choice; it was a mandate. It was an extraction of my agency, not a gift.

It’s a strange duality, isn't it? As a nurse, I’m the one who often has to advocate for the patient, to explain the "why," to soften the edges of the medical machine. I’ve seen the way colleagues of mine—perfectly kind, competent, well-meaning people—can accidentally steamroll a mother because they’re just so focused on the *goal* of a safe birth. I’ve done it, too, probably. But when you’re the one on the table, you realize the immense, terrifying power of the person holding the chart.

I still go back to work. I put on the scrubs, I check the monitors, and I walk into those rooms. But it’s different now. I don’t see the patient as just a medical record anymore. I see the history they’re carrying, the fear they’re suppressing, and the unspoken questions they’re too intimidated to ask. I try to be the bridge I never had. I try to listen for the "no" that’s hiding behind their "okay." 

I don’t know if I’ll ever truly "heal" from feeling like a spectator to my own life, but I think I’ve found a way to use that experience as a way to make sure the next person feels a little less alone than I did. It’s not much, but it’s something. I think that’s all we can do—try to be a little more human in a system that’s designed to be anything but.
What would trust in your care team look like at a moment like that — what would they need to do or say for you to feel genuinely supported?

Trust in a crisis isn't about them getting everything "right" on the first try; it’s about acknowledging my presence in the room. If I were in that same emergency today, trust would look like the OB stopping the cascade of noise for three seconds, looking directly into my eyes—not at the monitor, not at my chart—and saying, "Flo, the baby needs us to act fast. We are going to do X, Y, and Z. Do you have a question before we start?"

Those three seconds would change everything. It wouldn't stop the emergency, but it would move me from being a *specimen* to a *participant*. It would signal that my voice still holds weight, even in a room full of surgeons.

It’s the difference between being handled and being held. Genuine support would mean acknowledging that the *emotional* risk of the procedure is just as high as the clinical risk. If they had validated my fear instead of rushing past it, I wouldn't have felt like I was being erased. I would have felt like I was being cared for by a team that understood the magnitude of what they were asking of me. When a provider shows they can hold space for my humanity, they earn my trust—and in those moments, that trust is the only thing that makes the trauma survivable. They don't need to be perfect; they just need to be human.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

I think the only thing I’d add—and it’s something I keep coming back to as I pack away these onesies—is that we tend to treat the "postpartum" period like it’s just the physical recovery. We talk about the bleeding, the stitches, the healing of the incision, the breastfeeding, the sleep. But there’s this secondary, quiet, and often invisible recovery that happens—the recovery of the self.

I’m a different person than I was before I walked into that delivery suite. The nurse in me, the one who lived in the world of protocols and efficiency, had to reconcile with the woman who was reduced to a number on a chart. And that reconciliation is an ongoing process. It’s not something you "get over" in six weeks. It’s a permanent shift in how I view the medical system, how I view my own body, and how I view the people I work alongside every single day.

I really hope your research goes beyond just the outcomes. I hope you capture the stories of the women who felt silenced, the ones who felt like they had to "behave" to get the care they deserved. Because that silence? That’s where the real damage is done. If you can help the system understand that a woman’s birth experience is just as vital as her baby’s Apgar score, then maybe, just maybe, the next woman won't have to carry the same invisible weight I do.

Thank you for listening. It feels… lighter, just a little bit, to have said these things out loud. It makes the nursery feel a bit less like a place of anxiety and a bit more like a home.
